replies.title: (no subject)

Nah, music belongs to Venus, not to Mercury, so your first thing to do is see if you can get Venus in good condition (not in Aries, Scorpio, or Virgo, and not in negative aspect with Mars or Saturn), and then get the Moon applying to Venus by trine or sextile, while neither the Moon nor Venus is in square or opposition to anything or in conjunction with Mars or Saturn. Hello, and thanks for hosting these. I've been dealing with some writers block, and I have done the exercises that you've specifically outlined to help one deal with this issue. replies.title: Re: Mantra for the Blocked
replies.content: That's potentially workable. You could also try something more focused like "I've outgrown my writer's block."

replies.title: Re: Mantra for the Blocked
replies.content:
Dear anonymous,
I write too. Depending on your personal predilictions, you might benefit from the books of Julia Cameron and/or Dr. Eric Maisel.

replies.title: Re: Mantra for the Blocked
replies.content: May I ask for a link to the exercises for writers block? That could be useful for me. Thanks in advance!

replies.title: Re: Mantra for the Blocked
replies.content: The exercise is very simple. Every day, for 15 minutes by the clock, write. Do not let yourself stop writing even if all that comes out is "I don't know what to write, my mind is completely blocked, what should I write," and so on. Just keep the pen moving, or your fingers going on the keyboard -- and do not let yourself stop to correct anything you have written. Writer's block is a neurological issue; the part of your brain that creates and the part of your brain that edits are incapable of working at the same time, and when you try, your brain freezes up and you get writer's block. Do not edit while you're writing; don't even try to assess what you're writing while you're writing. Just barf it out onto the paper or the screen, save it, and edit it later -- that's how you get past writer's block once and for all.

replies.title: Re: Mantra for the Blocked
replies.content: That makes a TON of sense. Much appreciated!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The SoP was created in a time and place where pandemics were quite common. The United States in the 1950s had several large epidemics. There was an ongoing polio epidemic which peaked every summer until the vaccine was finally introduced in 1955. The tuberculosis epidemic of 1950 killed 34,000 people in the US, and the Asian flu pandemic of 1957 killed 62,000 people here. Most people alive then also remembered the widespread shutdowns and rationing during the Second World War. That being the case, the SoP is at least as well suited to our present situation as any ritual you care to name.

...and this is why I keep on pointing out that if you got your knowledge of history from a modern US public school education, you know nothing about history. I bet your teachers never mentioned any of those epidemics, did they?

replies.title: testing homeopathy
replies.content:
The major problem in testing homeopathy against allopathic methods is that homeopaths and allopaths are not talking about the same things when they describe disease. To take the common cold as an example. There is a range of symptoms and order of onset that constitutes 'having a cold' in most people's minds. If you have those symptoms you take whatever measures have worked in the past. A conventional doctor would confirm your diagnosis, if you were in a clinical trial for example, by testing for whichever rhinovirus was going around that season. A homeopath would listen to all your symptoms, including things that an allopath would never ask about, such as your dreams, which side you like to sleep on, whether you like warm or cool rooms. Then based on the totality of symptoms they would prescribe a remedy--one person with a 'cold' might get Allium because they presented with thin mucus and runny eyes, another might get a different remedy because they presented with a sore throat followed by thick green nasal mucus. To an allopath if both people have the same virus they have the same disease, and would be given the same treatment, to a homeopath they don't have the same disease at all. So how would you set up trial groups that would meet the standards of both systems?

As far as caffeine, it is my understanding that the problem is coffee, not caffeine. My homeopath has never forbidden tea or cola and has even recommended dark chocolate. However even decaffeinated coffee is a no-no. Fortunately I don't like coffee. Other forbidden substances include camphor and similar herbs, such as tea tree oil, as well as other influences such as electric blankets or heating pads and dental work. (Doesn't mean you let your teeth fall apart, just have to renew any constitutional remedy after being drilled.)

The major problem with homeopathy for most Americans is the expense--since they spend so much time on patients they must charge fairly high fees, especially for the initial appointment which may be as long as 1 1/2 to 2 hours. And insurance doesn't cover it. The remedies are quite inexpensive.

comments.title: The mystical art of muscle testing
comments.content:
Two Magic Monday’s ago Syfen replied to my post by asking what exactly muscle testing is.

Muscle testing, also known as applied kinesiology, is a technique used by various alternative health practitioners, most regularly by chiropractors. In a clinical setting it involves testing the strength of one of the patient’s large muscles (commonly the posterior deltoid or biceps femoris in a prone position) while touching or manipulating a point on the body. If the point being contacted is misaligned, injured, imbalanced, or otherwise problematic, touching it will weaken the entire organism, including the muscle being tested. This allows the practitioner to zero in on the exact disturbance that wants correction. Yes, the body wants to be in balance and wants disturbances rebalanced. In fact, sometimes an obvious problem will not test weak until some other problem that the body wants addressed first gets identified and corrected. Some bodies behave more willfully than others, and some seem downright cantankerous.

Many different kinds of imbalance can be tested. Meridians’ primary contact points can be muscle tested in set sequence to decode complex imbalances. Then the causative meridian’s points can be tested to identify which needs adjustment. Simple vertebral subluxations can be muscle tested easily. For more complex subluxations the bone can be tugged in the corrective direction (tests strong) or tugged in the direction of imbalance (tests even weaker.) Muscle disorders can be identified by pressing on the target muscle or by touching the muscle’s regulatory points on the torso and cranium while testing for body weakness. Further specificity can be gained by spreading the fingers apart or pinching together along the muscle body; whichever one weakens the body indicates over-release or over-contraction of the muscle.

Anyone can practice muscle testing themselves by having a friend start by testing the normal strength of a large muscle group (commonly the lateral deltoid while standing) followed by testing while either person touches suspected imbalances. When the muscle tests weaker, you have found a point where some kind of attention would strengthen the entire organism. What kind of attention? That’s where experience, training, and artistry come into play — there are as many types of imbalance and adjustment as there are healthcare modalities.

As for muscle testing as a mystical practice, the same idea of the whole organism weakening under any increased stress or disorganization can be applied to asking simple yes/no questions. A true/yes/balancing answer tests strong while a false/no/imbalancing answer tests weak. This is more difficult to practice than the contact point testing, which works on everyone. Because the stress/disorganization comes from holding a question, but not its answer, in your mind, you have to be able to get out of the way enough to think about the question but let your body answer it for you — no easy task! Some personalities try very hard to control the outcome and can’t let their body/instinct/subconscious respond at all. Also, if you don’t trust the friend testing your strength, especially for revealing questions, you may find it very difficult to access your body’s truth. Again artistry and experience come into play as triangulating in on food allergies, long forgotten injuries, or herbal remedies by only asking yes/no questions can be a complex dance. If you ask a vague question, your body may respond to its own understanding of it, which may be quite different from your conscious understanding!

Some people can practice muscle testing on themselves. Others find it too difficult to hold the muscle being tested at a constant resistance, the limb doing the testing under willful control, and the mind in a relaxed, pass-through-conduit state but still concentrating on a question. Although I have met people who test by trying to break a circle of their index finger and thumb, I have never found that technique to work well at all. Our finger muscles are under our active conscious control so much of the time that it becomes difficult to relinquish that control to let the body speak through them. I prefer testing the large muscle of my iliopsoas by pressing down on the thigh of my lifted leg.

Asking your body or subconscious for answers to questions is always an act of supplication, never of domination. Be very, very careful asking questions using muscle testing because your deeper self can become quite resentful if it feels you are trying to steal goodies from its cookie jar. That’s dark magic, and it will burn you badly. I worked with health care professionals using applied kinesiology for many years before tentatively attempting to use it myself for similar contact point work. It was years after that that I cautiously began using it for questions about my body and its health. Any time I am unsure whether a question is in my body’s interest I ask, “Is it OK to ask you this question?” When I get a no response, I do not ask it, period.

To reinforce that point I will conclude with a paragraph from the previous post: We all have far more contact and connection with our strangely intelligent and willful bodies and psyches than we tend to recognize. Our bodies are continually monitoring and adjusting our blood pressure, glandular secretions, chemical imbalances, temperature, immune responses, etc., etc., etc. Our bodies know so much that never comes into our conscious awareness, and, if we can figure out how to respect them enough to communicate with them as equals, they are happy to share that information. The body truly is a temple, complete with oracle. Our bodies’ goals like health and survival may be very different from our personalities’ goals like wealth and power, and they will certainly not share their truth with us if we try to control and manipulate them. They may well lie to us if we try, even to their own detriment. Never try to harness a wild thing; content yourself to follow alongside it wherever it is going.

Christophe

JMG, I’d like to ask a question, but it may be clearer if prefaced it with a story from Indian tradition – so here it goes.

One day a traditional healer saw a mysterious woman enter the village at dusk. As she came close to his house, he asked her who she was. “The goddess of cholera” was her reply. “Go about and do your business”, replied the healer, who then asked her, “how many of my village will you be taking?” The goddess said, “ten”. She continued to walk into the village. Soon after, cholera spread through the village, keeping the healer very busy. Eventually, one evening the healer saw the goddess leaving the village and he angrily accosted her: “You told me that you would take ten people of my village and fifty have died – why did you lie to me?” “I did not lie”, the goddess replied, “I took only ten… the other forty died of fear”.

comments.title: Rank Beginner
comments.content:
Mr Greer, I'd like to ask your advice and that of other commenters who might have experienced a similar situation to what follows.

I am a lifelong atheist who has never felt any spiritual tug, and have been frankly contemptuous of those who claimed to have experienced the divine. As I get older and more fully realize how vanishingly tiny my knowledge really is, I find myself increasingly drawn to try to experience the vast and rich worlds you and others describe. I have your Druidry Handbook (unread) and Mystery Teachings from the Living Earth (which I read along with on Ecosophia), as well as several others of your occult books.

My dilemma is two-fold:
1. I expect to feel deeply foolish and self-conscious for a time in starting any spiritual practice. How should I handle that?

2a. The greater issue for me is my home. My husband was brought up a born-again Christian but has fallen far from those beliefs, and while he won't call himself an atheist, he has no kinship with any belief system. My influence was instrumental in that. I have no desire to open myself to his gaze unless and until I become strong in a new path.

2b. I have no space in my house I can retreat to each day. My husband works a rotating shift, meaning that the hours he is at home vary from day to day and week to week, and I no longer work a paying job. Further, our dynamic by and large is dominated by his needs. My character tends to denigrate and doubt my needs (if I can even identify them).

How do I move forward? I feel as though I'm suffocating just writing this.

Thank you so much.

replies.title: Re: Rank Beginner
replies.content:
The answer will (IMHO) depend in part on how mutually respectful of one another and how open to one another you and your husband are. Can you talk over pretty much anything else together? If so, tell him what you just told us. His response may surprise you. Everyone does change over the decades of life, even one's husband, though it makes many people uncomfortable to acknowledge those natural changes. He, too, may be changing, and may not have known how to broach the subject of his own changes with you. He may even be changing in the same direction you are--that's a natural enough sort of change as one ages and draws ever nearer to one's own death. To paraphrase Samuel Johnson, "There is nothing that so focuses your mind as the certain knowledge and constant awareness that you will die much sooner than you like."

If not, if you are always somewhat cautions about opening up to one another about anything, then it might be easier to start by talking about being more open to one another in general with respect to change.

Or ... would even that *very clearly* be more than he could ever handle? Is he very rigid, and seems to hate to talk about anything at all profound, or to think that you and he might not agree on any point, however minor? Do you feel like you have to walk on eggshells with him? Then you can try to implement the desired changes in your own life while "flying under the radar." With his schedule so variable, "under the radar" means your schedule of practices, too, will need to be equally variable.

If that seems too hard for now, you could try working to change the "energy" in the house that you share, without letting him know what you're doing. Since it's your house, too, you do not (IMHO) need his consent to work on the house's "energy" by occult means such as hoodoo floor washes and so forth. (See our host's many accounts of such things in previous posts.) Just doing occult cleansing work of that sort on your shared house will *inevitably* change him as well as you, whether he would go along with it or not. It might even lead eventually to more open commnunication between the two of you.

What may also help you a lot just now is keeping in view that the sort of things we talk about here often do not really require any sort of "belief" (in the religious or spiritual sense) at all. Do you have to "believe" that your hammer will work in order to drive a nail with it, or can you just start hammering and see what happens? Me, I just start hammering. Do you have to "believe" in all the nutritional science out there to cook a meal that you'll both enjoy and will be good for you both? I'd wager that you just start cooking dinner. A lot of the things we talk about here require no more belief than hammering a nail does, or cooking a meal.

But be cautious, and keep in mind that there may be a personal cost even so. There are marriages that can't take even the least of the strains that are naturally caused by each partner's variable growth in wisdom, love and knowledge over their years together. The things you are now interested in will definitely change you, even if you do nothing more than read about them steadily for a number of years. So please do weigh the risks and the benefits to yourself beforehand. You can minimize those risks, but nothing you (or anyone) could do would ever make them go completely away. Can you live with those risks? And also, can you go on living without taking those particular risks?

Best wishes as you wrestle with all this stuff--it is a very hard road to travel!

comments.title: hidden motivations
comments.content:
A bit of a long story, but I'm inviting discussion from the commentariat and wondering if anyone else has had a similar experience within the current situation.

Where I live we've been encouraged to stay home and practice social distancing to slow the spread of the virus. During the first week I had a somewhat cavalier attitude toward this, and was still going out to visit with one friend or two each day. I am young and healthy and not afraid of catching coronavirus.

A couple of days ago my girlfriend suggested (over the phone) that we both be more careful and strict about not seeing people outside our homes, because she's started to be concerned about the risks to her aging parents. I was surprised by the strength of the feeling of resistance that arose in me at that moment, but I told her her request was reasonable and that I would limit my contacts until it seemed safe to do otherwise.

The next morning I went out for some (solitary) exercise, and in the course of my run I was chased for several hundred yards across the local golf course by an unfamiliar black dog. On my way home I encountered three more black dogs, one of which barked at me aggressively. A black dog has frequently figured in my dreams over the years, and I think I am ready to name what it means to me.

The fact is that I wasn't taking coronavirus prevention seriously because I've been secretly rooting for the virus, against the humans. For years I've watched environmental problems grow worse and wished there were less humans in the world. The notion of a pandemic always presented itself as the cleanest and simplest route to a cleaner and less overburdened world. Now it seems I've got my wish.

From a utilitarian perspective, someone who wished to slow environmental destruction would do well to go around licking doorknobs at this time- for the greater good! But I don't think morality works that way, or karma. What I realized is that I'd been letting my unconscious feelings draw me passively into harm's way- other people's harm, that is. And those feelings can be neatly summed up as a murderous hatred for humanity.

Well, that was nice to know. It explained why that day's Tarot spread had the Devil in the position of the significator (me!) and why that black dog has popped up so often in my dreams, and now in waking life. I've been lying to myself about the murderous rage that's been dogging me for years, pretending instead to be a nice guy who wants to help people.

1) To use the language of the Cosmic Doctrine, it seems I've fallen short of outright 'positive evil' in my lax attitude toward the virus, but I have allowed myself to be passively pulled into the current of Chaos, rather than stepping completely aside and letting it pass me by. From a karmic perspective, though, is it accurate to say that there's no real difference, and that I've risked terrible consequences for myself and others?

2) How can I discharge this huge weight of hatred and rage that I now realize I've been carrying around? Journaling has helped bring it to light (hence this post), but what other steps can I take to be free of it?

replies.title: Re: hidden motivations
replies.content:
I don't think you are alone in this. Since the first edition of _The Population Bomb_ (1968) we have been steeped in messages that there are too many people, that we are destroying the planet and so on. You would have to have lived in a cave not to have absorbed and internalized that message. For Westerners this may be intensified by our Christian cultural background--Sinners in the Hands of an Angry God--the message that all humans, even those who appear to be living righteous lives are miserable vermin in the eyes of their Creator is part of our culture. This sermon appears in literature anthologies for American literature survey courses, so even if you were not raised as a Christian you could have encountered it.

Rita

replies.title: Re: hidden motivations
replies.content:
1) There's a difference from a karmic perspective, because the actions are different. Deliberately willing harm is one thing, letting yourself be drawm passively into a pattern that causes harm is another -- but both have very unwelcome karmic consequences.

2) You've taken the crucial step -- a step a vast number of people are still refusing to take -- in admitting that you're not a "nice guy," you really do have all this hatred and rage inside of you. So does everyone else. As I pointed out a while ago in a blog post, today's attitude toward hatred and rage is identical to the Victorian attitude toward sexual desire; most of us pretend that we don't feel it, that only those awful people over there ("hate groups") would ever indulge in it ("hate speech"), and so our psyches are festering swamps of repressed hatred and rage that seep out in various more or less destructive ways we go out of our way not to notice. Acknowledge your hatred and rage, accept that these are normal, necessary, healthy human emotions, let yourself feel them, and you can release the repressions in a controlled, conscious, and constructive way.

comments.title: discursive meditation
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

I have a couple questions about proper discursive meditation, which I have been practicing for about a year.

1. I’m a professional writer of non-fiction. The quality and texture of discursive meditation seems very similar to what happens when I conceive and arrange a long essay or story. Similarly to a trial attorney developing a case, or a member of the clergy devising a good sermon. One must explore all angles of the topic, and then weave them into a coherent whole. Attorneys, clergy and professional writers often take a certain angle on the topic, of course; if you don’t, it renders the end result almost meaningless. Is this a good understanding of discursive meditation, or are there aspects I am missing?

2. The four-fold breath is tricky to maintain when following a long course of thought. Sometimes I manage it, but counting in the background is effortful. Sometimes it does seem like the breath helps the thoughts…travel. Is there something to this? Or should I stop worrying about the breath timing when I start meditating on my chosen theme? (Usually I practice four-fold breath alone for five or six minutes, and then meditate for five or six minutes.)

3. Occasionally when I try to start the breath practice I get really hot and my breath rate speeds up and it gets uncomfortable to continue. Does this indicate anything in particular, or just that today isn’t the day to meditate?

Sincerely,

Ms. Krieger

replies.title: Re: discursive meditation
replies.content:
1) That's a very good start. There are other aspects to discursive meditation, but they unfold over time with practice. If you start with the understanding you have, you'll have good results.

2) You don't keep doing the fourfold breath while you meditate. You do the fourfold breath for five minutes or so first, to clear your mind, then you change to ordinary quiet breathing and begin the meditation proper.

3) Are you by any chance menopausal? If so, try imagining that the heat is energy, and the energy flows through your feet and diffuses itself in the ground. My wife also found that a good way to respond to hot flashes.

replies.content: Thank you for your considered answers. I’ll keep meditating with this in mind. (And not menopausal yet, but I will experiment with grounding the energy now and keep the technique in mind for the future.)

1. I’m having some trouble grasping exactly what the five classical elements (fire, water, air, earth, quintessence) are. I assume these are not simply the building blocks of matter like a primitive form the periodic table, right?

In Learning Ritual Magic, you cover them in a single sentence: “The five elements are not static, unchanging counters. They are moments in a process of transformation.” I’ve been mulling over this sentence for 2 or 3 months and still don’t understand what it means. Can you offer some clues?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) The five elements are modes of manifestation. They occur on every plane. You've doubtless encountered people who are free spirits, people who are fiery, people who are airheads, people who are all wet, and people who are really down to earth, right? Those are the four elements on the plane of personality. On every other plane of being, you'll find things that fit into the same categories. They all flow into one another, the way that ice (which is solid, i.e., earth) melts into liquid water and then evaporates into vapor (i.e., air) -- a complete airhead can get down to earth, though you're likely to see an emotional outburst with a lot of tears on the way!

2) What interest do human beings have in pets -- or, for that matter, in service animals?

3) It's a valid question, but the answer requires a bit of occult philosophy. Human beings have bodies on the material, etheric, and astral planes -- the planes of physical substance, life force, and concrete consciousness, which includes the emotions and the imagination. On the mental plane, the next plane up, we are still evolving bodies -- what we have at this point is a mental sheath, a sort of rudimentary structure that over many lives will evolve into a mental body. Psychism works through the mental plane, and the more developed your mental sheath becomes, the more psychic capacities you have.

Desires, however, belong to the lower end of the astral plane -- and yes, this includes the desire for wealth, and the desire for anything you can get with wealth. The more you focus on your desires, the less attention you can pay to the mental plane, and so the weaker your psychism becomes. So as long as you're trying to win a lottery payout, and are fixated on that desire, your psychism isn't going to work well.

This is why many beginning diviners can do good divinations for other people but get lousy results when they try to cast a reading for themselves. You can get past that, but it takes plenty of practice and a certain amount of calmness about the issues that affect you directly!

As a former card-carrying member of the cult of rationalism, one way that I can be reconciled with astrology is that, relating it to Jung, it serves as a way to bring contents that are already present in the unconscious mind into consciousness. It is our own intuition about things that is generally correct, and can be honed with practice, but this intuition is primordial and difficult to express in language, which developed at a much later stage of human evolution, so it is through techniques of divination that we can tap into it and give it a voice. Moreover, to the chagrin of rationalists, these intuitions are not “scientific,” and don’t fit neatly into an experimental or empirical paradigm.

It's not an empty void waiting for our minds to fill it -- there's plenty happening there all the time, and very often the thoughts that "enter our heads" do exactly that, and come from outside. Hello Archdruid,

Is it true that Knights Templar revered Mahomed (given their relations with Ismaili Order of Assassins), and their persecutors mistook it as "Baphomet"?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
No, there's something much subtler going on with that name. In traditional Jewish Cabala, it's common to conceal holy names using various ways of encoding, and one of the simplest is called the "Atbash cipher." What that word means is that you flip the Hebrew alphabet, so that the first letter and the last letter are exchanged, the second and the next to last, and so on. Thus Aleph is replaced by Tau and Tau by Aleph, Beth is replaced by Shin and Shin by Beth. So if you write Baphomet in Hebrew -- Beth, Peh, Vau, Mem, Tau -- and you put it though the Atbash cipher, you get Shin, Vau, Peh, Yod, Aleph...which is the Hebrew spelling of the name of Sophia, the Gnostic goddess of wisdom.

That was the secret of the Templars. They encountered, most likely in the Holy Land, one of the surviving Gnostic sects, and adopted that as their secret teaching. I'm pretty sure, for reasons Jessie Weston covered in her book From Ritual to Romance, that it was either a Naassene sect or something similar -- one that saw Christian orthodoxy as incomplete rather than wrong. But that's why the Catholic church turned with such savagery on the Templars: the Church had just finished committing genocide against one set of Gnostics, the Cathars, in southern France, and all of a sudden another set of Gnostics popped up right in the heart of the Church's most powerful military order. (Of course the greed of Philip IV of France also had a lot to do with the fate of the Templars; it was, as most historical events were, overdetermined.)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: There's some reason to believe that they did so in Scotland and Germany, at least, but for obvious reasons it was kept very quiet.

One thing to keep in mind is that when a lineage goes extinct, it's sometimes possible to pick it up on the inner planes and bring the old energy back down into manifestation again. That's pretty clearly what happened with the system of magic taught in my book The Celtic Golden Dawn -- its rituals have much more kick to them than a newly invented set of rituals should have, and I'm pretty sure that what's behind that is that I got close enough to the workings used by the old Druid/Golden Dawn hybrid orders that the contacts were picked up and the energies of those older traditions flowed into the new one.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
JMG,

I was the commenter one or two weeks ago who asked you about the problem of evil, and how different prophetic religions like Christianity, Buddhism, and the Gnostic movement dealt with it. Your response was that the problem of evil is only a problem for those who have a pre-conception of what the universe ought to be, and find themselves disappointed as a result, and that the likes of Siddharta Guatama were basically privileged whiners.

If I understand correctly, your position implies a radical acceptance of the world as it is, warts and all - with all the evil and cruelty of human nature, and the seeming indifference of the natural world towards our wellbeing. But this leaves open the question of why suffering exists, on a spiritual level. The materialist answer is easy: random chance combined with evolutionary selection created animals that are self-interested and optimized to win in the struggle for survival, not for any higher concept like justice. Nietzche famously called Christian morality "slave morality," and claimed that it is upheld only by those who are too weak to satisfy the will-to-power and become heroic ubermenschen (ironically, Nietzsche himself was an invalid).

As I'm sure you're aware, it doesn't take too many leaps of logic from here, despite Hume's concept of the is-ought gap, to arrive at Social Darwinism, exemplified (in fiction) by the likes of Gordon Gekko and Patrick Bateman, and in real life by countless imitators. I'm sure that many captains of industry and "leaders" currently at the helm of the United States have privately, if implicitly, adopted this as their philosophy of life, and behave accordingly. After all, if these are truly the rules of the game, why wouldn't individuals optimize their strategy accordingly? If we live in a Darwinian universe, even a concern for ethics or justice can only be another power play, a strategy among others to advance one’s genes, as valid as any other.

I think that Schopenhauer's attitude to suffering is much more appropriate and humane, that it is the fundamental evil and a moral tragedy, and that life itself is evil unless this world can be redeemed somehow. If materialism is correct in that there is no higher plane or moral oversight, then this life is basically the closest approximation of the Christian Hell. The only escape is the possibility that we are here for a particular reason, to serve out a punishment or to learn some harsh lessons for reasons that must remain unknown to us, at this stage. Perhaps this earthly life is a kind of trial or penitentiary, as Schopenhauer said, intended as a purging by fire before we can rise to the next level? But I essentially have no evidence for this view, so it remains an absurd leap of faith, an empty consolation.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
For what it's worth, I devoted two of my novels -- The Shoggoth Concerto and The Nyogtha Variations -- to exactly this theme. The basic argument, to put things the way that shoggoths do, is that "the universe has no eyes, but we have eyes." In less gnomic terms, it's absurd for us to ask why there's suffering in the cosmos, or why anything at all is in the cosmos; we have about as much chance of figuring out those things that the dust mites in Isaac Newton's wig had of understanding his Principia Mathematica; the universe does what it does, without reference to human moral notions; it neither loves us nor hates us, it is wholly indifferent to us, and a robust acceptance of that reality is the foundation of human sanity.

We are not the cosmos, however. Unlike the cosmos, we are capable of perceiving the moral consequences of our actions and managing our actions accordingly. Hume's is-ought gap is essential to sanity here -- the fact that the cosmos as a whole behaves in a certain way does not place any obligation on us to act that way, or for that matter to refrain from acting in that way. Ethics, in this way of thinking, is the sum total of human knowledge about how to live well; it's not a set of rules handed down from on high, it's an ongoing exploration, fallible and incomplete but a lot better than no guidance at all.

I find much to admire in Schopenhauer's epistemology and ontology, but the practical side of his ethics can be tested by checking his own biography -- "by their fruits ye shall know them" is good advice -- and his insistence that life is misery and woe clashes entertainingly with his lifelong passion for gourmet food, music, and prostitutes. Recognizing that ethics applies only to conscious intelligent beings such as humans -- and shoggoths! -- and that the universe is as amoral and as lovely as a waterfall or a storm provides a much saner approach to living well and dying well.

comments.title: Divination and my Relationship
comments.content:
Hello JMG!

(Although this is heavily related to divination, I think this is also falls very hard into the "Give me life advice" category and if you feel like it does too much so and isn't related enough to occultism I completely understand abstaining from answering.)

Some necessary background, I've been learning and using the I ching and the Tarot since last August. (In conjunction with a Kraig's Modern Magick style ceremonial magick practice, meditation, LBRP, MP, etc) I mostly did divinations as needed, not as a daily practice where I re-evaluated each day. I think I'm at a low level of competence.

I am currently in a romantic relationship, that has been going very well, we were friends for years previously, but in the last few months became romantically involved, and got serious fairly quickly.


In the last couple days my divinations in regard to our relationship have been turning more negative.

This could totally be one of those classic blunders where there's one thing you're extremely afraid of and keep finding it in every reading, but of course I want a second opinion.

I use a tarot phone app fairly frequently, and that's what started me on this subject. When I asked for an oracle on our relationship I pulled the devil.

Which in some interpretations could be positive I suppose, binding together in relationship, coming into being, but I also generally read the devil as expressly negative.

After this I switched to the I ching, as I find it generally harder to make readings what you want them to be (as opposed to what they likely actually mean) especially when lots of emotion is involved.

I asked the question "what do I most meet to learn in my relationship with hername", cast it, and got the response inner truth changing to limitation.

With the changing line "cockrow penetrating to heaven perseverance brings misfortune" wilhelms commentary that stuck out to me being "A man may count on mere words to awaken faith, this may succeed now and then, but if persisted in, will have bad consequences.

For a long time I have been curious about non-monogamous relationship arrangement's, and was open with my girlfriend about this when we started our romantic relationship. I said I didn't know if I was monogamous or wanted to be committed, and the only thing I could promise is I would always be honest and tell her how I felt. We broke up for a short while because of this, because I brought up the fact that I was still curious about dating other people about a month and a half into our relationship. This lead to a temporary split up (1 week ish) that was extremely painful, and I decided I just wanted to be with her as a life partner, as the other kinds of relationships I was interested in weren't as important to me as this primary one, and I choose her with commitment.

This breakup also caused her a lot of pain, and caused a lot of confusion for me, because of the diverging feelings I felt at that time. I really struggled with what I felt like I was "supposed" to do at that time, (Supposed to in order to fulfill my highest self and be the best person I can be) and eventually settled on, in a very intentional way, the only way to be committed or to feel comfortable in a relationship despite my fears is to choose this person over those fears over and over again, and to accept that, and laugh as much as I can at all the uncertainty, and simply do my best.

My girlfriend know's what she wants, which is a committed monogamous relationship, eventually with children, to pursue writing, to be of benefit to the community, etc.

I am 21, she is about the same age, and as you do at this age I've been trying to figure out what the best thing to do with my life is, especially via divination, contemplation, etc.

At this moment I really want the kind of life I can see us having together. She's really beautiful, funny, accepting of my spirituality, we have the same views on a lot of things and have mature understandings of the things we disagree on, we work well together in practical ways, enjoy each others company, and I think she's a really great person, I am interested in having children, she also supports my life goals (Learn and practice sustainable farming, continue practicing magick and developing spiritually etc)

So Wilhem's commentary about mere words awakening faith touched on a fear I already have in myself, that I am terrible, that my words of commitment are meaningless.

I am so scared that I'm not gonna be right for her in some way, having made a commitment to myself and her, or that our relationship won't work out. Mostly because I fear inflicting a ton of pain on her and her family, and it being my fault, and also being scorned because of it. Selfish and unselfish motivations all in one, in this highly emotional pattern that ties in with patterns I've recognized occurring since early childhood. Some of it is self negativity and over ascribing everything to being my fault, in order to avoid responsibility. (because if I'm so bad I'm not responsible for fixing what I'm not "able", according to this narrative, to fix) If I don't like her or the relationship doesn't work out in my head, it's automatically my fault, I've failed her and everyone, that's the way it feels emotionally, although I can recognize it as what it is at least to this degree.

And to be clear I am committed to her in my words and actions for the foreseeable future, but not yet committed to marriage. And I'd say the only specific times I've been feeling uncommitted in heart and mind is recently in this fear and paranoia around these readings.

Something I've picked up recently in conjunction with the use of the I ching is flipping one of the coins I use for it to get answers to yes or no questions. When I asked the I ching for an oracle on continuing to do this I got Treading (Right Conduct) transitioning to Innocence, changing line (Walking a smooth even path, perseverance of a dark man, brings good fortune) I interpreted it as it being okay to continue this practice. (When I asked after a lot of this happened)

Being in a state of heightened emotion, knowing the thing I was most afraid of: I asked "Are you suggesting that I break up with hername" the answer came back heads, yes.

Further coin tosses, and divinations gave me more to think about, I'm shortly going to be moving in with her, and it gave me a positive indication on that. Coin tosses answering yes or no questions also seemed to indicate to me that if we did break up it would not be my fault, and this issue would not stem from me but from her, although I have no idea what this issue could be, although I was given some answers on perhaps why, but I doubt my ability to impartially interpret anything in this case.

I was visiting her later the same day, and talked to her about all of this. She put to me the very common sense question of "Are you going to break up with me solely based on divinations?" I said no. I wouldn't make a major hard life decisions based on divinations unless it confirms something I feel inside myself previously or whatever the divination is pointing to becomes reflected in my experience of material reality.

She told me she feels like she can trust me completely, and really wants to pursue a long term stable relationship, and eventually get married. She's not worried about it. I love her, and I don't want to disappoint her.

And for the record, I have no conscious issues with our relationship, no logical problems I can think of, except for my own neurosis around messing up and not being good enough, or not being able to fulfill our dreams together, and it being my fault, or it somehow in some way I can't define being opposed to the development of my higher self. I often look to divination as a guide for that subject.

We did a tarot pull together with a physical deck of hers, and when she pulled a card on "how our relationship will go" she pulled queen of pentacles.

Her divination on the subject of our relationship seem to be neutral (four of swords, single card draw) to positive (Queen of pentacles).

While mine, to this day, a couple days after that, remain negative.

I ask the tarot questions like "give me an oracle on marrying hername" and get answers like 10 of swords, or the devil. I certainly see parralells between the Inner truth, changing to Limitation, and the devil card. Or similar things when asking "Does my higher self want me to marry hername?" things like the 10 of swords, and if I ask if my higher self wants me to leave, I get the hermit. Or if I simply ask what my higher self wants me to do, I also get the hermit. Which I could either interpret as, go off alone, be a hermit, or as make your own decision according to your own truth.

When I interpret it as there will be some intense suffering in the future if I marry her or continue the relationship, please give me an oracle on marrying hername/going through that difficulty anyway, I get answers like the hierophant and temperance. I know I have my free will to choose. I don't want to break up with her, but there's also a part of me that's worried my stubbornness in this will somehow lead to spiritual stagnation, or not fulfilling my highest self because of my decision to stay. That part of me is like "Don't be a coward! Give up everything else for spiritual development, that's what matters! If the cards say it it will be better for everyone in the end!"

Is this simply a reflection of my inner state of fear and paranoia at this moment? Is that why her divinations are different? Could it simply be me being not a skilled enough diviner to see what is actually going on? Is your take that I am being a competent diviner and I'm clearly being told that there will be some misfortune in the relationship unless I leave?

Any idea what's going on here? am I just psyching myself out?

(I also did a ritual intended to help her, with her permission the night before all this started, but I don't want to extend this post longer and I don't see a direct correlation between the ritual and this)

Thanks for reading that very long account, and I truly appreciate your advice,

-WM

replies.title: Re: Divination and my Relationship
replies.content: Set aside your divinations. It sounds to me as though you've gotten into an internal echo chamber where your fears are generating feedback loops; that's something that happens, and it's one of the reasons a lot of older books tell you never to try to do readings for yourself. My recommendation is that you put your tarot cards and your I Ching in a box and leave them there for at least a year, and if you need oracular advice, ask someone else for a reading during that time.

replies.title: Re: Divination and my Relationship
replies.content:
Thank you. That's sound advice.

I was also the commenter under a similar name a few weeks ago that asked about transitioning to the CGD from more traditional GD practices, and how it may combine with Hatha Yoga. Would you also recommend I abstain from Geomancy, and don't proceed past it in the courses until the same time frame has passed?

-WM

comments.title: Grounding
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I've been using your advice on affirmations (100x/day) and very basic meditation (up to 10 min so far) for about 5 months now, with very good results. I do these exercises every workday morning, and I've definitely noticed a change in my overall mental, and a huge reduction in some recurring negative thoughts that I had. Just wanted to throw that out there for a data point, and offer you my sincerest gratitude.

I am also curious to know your thoughts on the exercise of grounding, ie putting your bare skin on the earth. My mother practices it and I've heard lots about it. I'm curious what you think of the science behind it (valid/fake/misunderstood?) and also if there is an etheric or astral explanation beyond the material.

Secondly, if you do think grounding is a beneficial exercise, is skin to ground contact important, or can you get the same benefits through a layer of clothing? I always hear that it has to be skin, but I can't think why my pants would inhibit any energy exchange if I'm sitting directly on the ground.

I hope you and everyone else are staying safe during this time. My thoughts and prayers go out to everyone here.

Cym

replies.title: Re: Grounding
replies.content: You're most welcome. Grounding is useful stuff; it puts you into contact with the telluric current, one of the two main currents of subtle energy. It's best done with bare skin, especially the soles of your feet (fairly easy to arrange) or the base of your spine (not so easy unless you can visit a nudist colony or a nude beach), but there's some benefit through clothing that's made of natural fibers -- cotton is fairly permeable.

comments.title: Opening ritual
comments.content:
Hey jmg

When doing the opening and closing ritual do you need to do the cabalistic cross 4x for the beginning and end of each LBRP or is one cross at the start of the 1st LBRP and another at the end of the 2nd enough?

replies.title: Re: Opening ritual
replies.content: You do it four times, once at the beginning and end of each LBRP. Make a point of concentrating on it and doing it as powerfully as you can; it's not just a formality, it can become a really powerful magical rite all by itself if you put serious effort and concentration into it.
